coredata.dc:title: Polydopamine-coated carbon nanodots are a highly selective turn-on fluorescent probe for dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Chaiendoo, Kanokwan
Ittisanronnachai, Somlak
Promarak, Vinich
Ngeontae, Wittaya
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared by the pyrolysis of lactose in hot alkali solution for 5 min. The resulting CDs possess spherical and monodisperse shape with an average diameter of ∼3 nm. The blue fluorescence of the CDs, with excitation/emission peaks at 414/470 nm, can be turned-on in the presence of very low concentrations of dopamine (DA) due to the formation of a poly-DA shell on the CDs. Fluorescence is only enhanced by DA, while other chemically related compounds do not interfere. Fluorescence increases linearly in the 1.0–10.0 μM DA concentration range, with a 0.22 μM lower detection limit. The nanoprobe was applied to the determination of DA in injections and in spiked urine samples, and the results compared well with those obtained by HPLC. The nanoprobe has a fast response, is inexpensive and highly selective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical sensors
Fluorescence sensors
Nanoparticles
Carbon nanostructures
Diagnosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Signal response and possible sensing mechanism
Characteristics of the analytical performance of the method
Measuring DA in a real urine sample and dopamine injection solution
Comparison of the performance characteristics of the DA nanoprobe with those of other detection platforms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund ( RSA6080006 and RTA6080005 ), the Thailand Research Fund (TRF) through the Royal Golden Jubilee (RGJ) Ph.D. Program (Grant No. PHD/0081/2557 ) to K.C. and W.N., Research and Technology Transfer Affairs of Khon Kaen University and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC) , Office of the Higher Education Commission , Ministry of Education .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectrum (dashed line) and fluorescence spectrum (solid line) of the synthesized CDs in DI water; (b), (e) HRTEM images of CDs and CDs + DA (c), (f) the enlarged HRTEM images of the CDs (b) and (e), respectively (d) the corresponding size distribution histogram of (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (black line), CDs in the presence of 10 μM of DA (red line), and polydopamine nanoparticles (PDA) prepared from 200 μM of DA (blue line) and 10 μM of DA (green line) in Tris-HCl buffer pH 8.0; (b) FT-IR spectra of DA, CDs, CDs + DA, PDA and the CDs + PDA; (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs, PDA and CDs + DA.
Illustration of possible sensing mechanism. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs in the presence of 10 μM of possible interfering compounds measured in 0.3 M Tris-HCl buffer pH 8.0; (b) corresponding degrees of fluorescent enhancement from various possible interfering compounds. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of DA measured in 0.3 M Tris-HCl buffer pH 8.0; (b) the corresponding calibration plot. The fluorescence intensities were measured at λem/λex 470/414 nm and the degree of fluorescent enhancement (F-F0) at 470 were used for construct the calibration plot. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Determination of DA in urine sample and DA injection solution using the proposed method and HPLC technique (n = 3).
Comparison of the performance characteristics of the DA nanoprobe with those of other detection platforms.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), gold nanorods (AuNRs), graphene sheets (GS) and 3,4,9,10 perylenetetra-carboxylic acid (PTCA), Au nanoparticle-decorated polypyrrole/reduced graphene oxide hybrid sheets (Au@PPy/GS), polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots (PPy/GQDs) core/shell hybrids, copper nanoparticles (CuNPs).

head.ce:title: Polydopamine-coated carbon nanodots are a highly selective turn-on fluorescent probe for dopamine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared by the pyrolysis of lactose in hot alkali solution for 5 min. The resulting CDs possess spherical and monodisperse shape with an average diameter of ∼3 nm. The blue fluorescence of the CDs, with excitation/emission peaks at 414/470 nm, can be turned-on in the presence of very low concentrations of dopamine (DA) due to the formation of a poly-DA shell on the CDs. Fluorescence is only enhanced by DA, while other chemically related compounds do not interfere. Fluorescence increases linearly in the 1.0–10.0 μM DA concentration range, with a 0.22 μM lower detection limit. The nanoprobe was applied to the determination of DA in injections and in spiked urine samples, and the results compared well with those obtained by HPLC. The nanoprobe has a fast response, is inexpensive and highly selective.

A new fluorescence sensor for DA is demonstrated by utilizing the optical properties of CDs synthesized from the pyrolysis of lactose. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs can be effectively enhanced in the presence of trace levels of DA due to the generation of polydopamine coated on the CD particles.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most crucial catecholamine neurotransmitters that control human physiological functions [1]. It plays significant roles in contacting the nervous system as well as in the renal and hormonal systems [2]. DA is a compound that is very sensitive to reacting with oxygen, especially in an alkaline environment (pH > 7.5) [3,4]. In a high-pH solution, DA will be oxidized by oxygen to provide its quinone derivative, and then the reaction will proceed to self-polymerize to yield polydopamine (PDA) [4–6]. The structure of PDA is very similar to those of natural eumelanin polymers [7]. PDA is composed of dihydroxyindole, indoledione, and dopamine units, which are assumed to be covalently linked [8]. However, some researchers have reported that the quinone product (produced by the oxidation of DA in an alkaline medium) is unstable and rapidly polymerized by covalent attachment and aggregation [4,9,10]. Although the actual structure of PDA is not well understood, DA is first oxidized by oxygen and then undergoes cyclization and rearrangement to produce the intermediate 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI) before undergoing polymerization [11].

PDA is a bioinspired material with tremendous potential for various applications; in particular, it has the potential to coat various surfaces to improve some properties of the coated materials [11]. PDA coating is one of the simplest and most versatile approaches to coating [12]; therefore, it has been demonstrated to functionalize a wide array of material surfaces. This may be due to PDA containing both catechol and amine groups, and it was discovered to be a facile surface coating strategy in 2007 [4]. PDA is hydrophilic, and it can adhere strongly to various inorganic and organic materials in a single step by mixing them with a dopamine solution [4,8,11,13–15]. Thus, it has been demonstrated that PDA can be used to coat metals, oxides, ceramics, polymers, carbon nanotubes, and magnetite nanoparticles [4,16–20]. PDA coating is very resistant and has attracted interest due to its promising biomedical applications [21]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no study has focused on using the PDA coating of carbon nanodots (CDs) to improve fluorescent emissions.

CDs have attracted considerable interest because they possess distinct advantages, such as multicolor emissions, high photostability, low toxicity, and biocompatibility; additionally, they do not require scarce elements and can easily be produced from a wide range of raw materials compared with organic dyes and other semiconductor nanodots with heavy metal cores [22–24]. Based on their distinct optical features, CDs have been employed for the fabrication of fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of target analytes via various sensing mechanisms. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports on the utilization of CDs in the fabrication of fluorescent nanoprobes for DA detection. Huang, H. and co-workers proposed a fluorescent nanoprobe for DA by using the hybrid system between CDs and the enzyme tyrosinase via the fluorescent quenching mode due to the excellent electron-accepting properties of the generated dopaminechrome [25]. In addition, Meng, X. and co-workers reported the use of CDs synthesized from a hot solution of citric acid and ethylenediamine for the fabrication of DA via a fluorescent quenching strategy [26]. They explained that the fluorescent quenching of CDs occurred based on the generation of DA quinone under alkaline conditions. Yang, Y. and co-workers synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots using a hot polyacrylamide solution [27]. The synthesized CDs can be utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of DA via the fluorescent quenching method because DA is transformed into dopaminequinone under alkaline conditions. Siddlingeshwar, B. and co-workers demonstrated CDs synthesis from the microwave-assisted process of ascorbic acid as a carbon source [28]. The prepared CDs can be used to detect DA via a fluorescent quenching approach. Furthermore, Zhou, S. and co-workers demonstrated a fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for the detection of DA-based CDs that was prepared from a hot solution of 3-aminophenylboronic acid [29]. They proposed that the enhanced fluorescence may stem from the specific interaction between DA and bifunctionalized carbon dots (B(OH)2 and NH2). George, S. and co-workers reported that nitrogen-doped carbon dots functionalized with aminophenylboronic acid can be used to fabricate fluorescent nanoprobes for DA detection [30]. This detection principle was based on the measurement of the fluorescent enhancement of the nanoprobe due to the formation of a boronate ester bridge between the carbon dots and DA. From the literatures, it can be seen that DA has not been detected via a fluorescent turn-on strategy via the formation of a polydopamine shell on CDs. In addition, no studies have utilized the polymerization of DA on CDs for the detection of DA by improving their fluorescent emission.

Basically, the main drawback of the fluorescent sensors based on the measurement of the fluorescent quenching is its selectivity. There are many of possible species that can affect to the fluorescent emission. On the other hand, there are a few species that can enhance the fluorescent emission. Therefore, the fluorescent sensor based turn-on strategy may provide better selectivity and possible be used in real situations than the fluorescent quenching assay. In addition, this work reported a new strategy for fabrication of the turn-on fluorescent sensor by the generation of PDA on the CDs with excellence selectivity and has not been previously reported.

In this work, we demonstrated a selective and sensitive fluorescence nanoprobe for the detection of DA based on the fluorescent enhancement of the synthesized CDs. The CDs were simply prepared in one step from the pyrolysis of lactose. The detection principle was based on the improvement in the fluorescent emission of the synthesized CDs, which resulted from the oxidation of DA in an alkaline medium followed by spontaneous polymerization on the CD surface. The sensing mechanism was elucidated using appropriate techniques. The parameters that may affect the detection sensitivity were studied and optimized in detail. The selectivity over other related structural compounds and possible interferences in real samples was demonstrated. Moreover, the feasibility of using the nanoprobe in real applications was assessed by using it to detect DA in urine sample and dopamine injection sample.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Lactose was obtained from QReC. Dopamine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide was obtained from UNIVAR. Tris-hydroxymethyl-methylamine was obtained from Merck. Hydrochloric acid (37%) was purchased from QReC. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm from RiOs™ Type I Simplicity 185 (Millipore water). The CDs were synthesized using a modified version of a previously published procedure [31] as the detail in Electronic Supporting Information (ESI).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu). The slit widths used for both excitation and emission were 5 nm. The absorption spectra of the synthesized CDs were recorded on an Agilent HP spectrophotometer. Bright field high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed with a JEOL JEM ARM200F transmission electron microscope operating at 200 kV. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the prepared CDs were obtained using a Tecnai G2-20 (FEI, Netherlands) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed with a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments, FS5). The pH values of the solutions were measured with a UB-10 Ultra Basic pH meter (Denver Instrument). The Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using Bruker TENSOR27 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy.

The applicability of the nanoprobe was demonstrated by the determination of DA in real urine sample. Fresh urine sample (10 mL) was analyzed within 1 h. The urine sample was centrifuged for 30 min at 6000 rpm before use to remove possible particles. For the analysis of the DA content of the dopamine injection sample, each sample was first diluted 2000-fold with deionized water. Then, 200 μL of the sample was added into a 10 mL volumetric flask containing 200 μL of CDs. The mixture reached a final volume of 10.00 mL with 0.3 M Tris-HCl buffer at a pH of 8.0. Each mixture solution was then incubated at room temperature for 20 min before its fluorescence spectrum was recorded. The fluorescence intensity was measured at λem/λex 470/414 nm. The fluorescence intensity values of the CDs were assigned as F0. The fluorescence intensity after adding DA (or samples) was assigned as F. The different intensities (F-F0) were plotted against the concentrations of DA to obtain a calibration plot. The concentration of DA can be calculated using the standard calibration plot. The accuracy and precision of each measurement were evaluated by spiking the urine sample at two different concentrations of DA (2 and 4 μM) and analyzed under the optimized conditions.

To confirm the accuracy of the method, the same samples and their spiked samples were analyzed by HPLC. The HPLC experiment was performed using the following previously reported conditions [32]. Briefly, an Inertsil ODS-3250 × 4.60 mm column (GL Science Inc., Japan) was equipped with an LC-20AD pump system. Peaks were detected and analyzed at 254 nm by an SPD-M20A photodiode array detector. The mobile phase consisted of 20 mM phosphate buffer at a pH of 2.5. HPLC separation was achieved using isocratic elution. The flow rate of the mobile phase was 1.0 mL min−1, while the injection volume was 20 μL. The concentration of DA in the samples was identified by comparison with the retention time of the DA standard, while the concentrations of DA were calculated using the external standard method.

The time resolved experiments were carried out by a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments) using the TCSPC (time correlated single photon counting) method. The excitation source was a pulsed 482 nm, and the photons were collected at 580 nm. The deconvolution and exponential fitting were performed using the Fluoracle® as the operating software.

The selectivity of the nanoprobe towards DA detection over those of other possible physiological compounds and ions, such as aniline, melamine, urea, lactose, fructose, alanine (Ala), leucine (Leu), L-phenylalanine (Phe), epinephrine, norepinephrine, ascorbic acid, glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), Na+, Zn2+, and Ca2+, was evaluated using the following procedure. In a 10 mL volumetric flask, an appropriate volume of the stock solution of the possible interference compounds was mixed with 200 μL of the prepared CDs. Then, 3 mL of 1 M Tris-HCl buffer at a pH of 8.0 was subsequently added to control the solution pH, and the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 10 mL with deionized water. The solution mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 20 min before its fluorescence spectrum was recorded.

The surfaces of the optical nanoparticles are very sensitive to changes in their surfaces, especially while they are adsorbed by other compounds. A change in fluorescence, including turn-on or turn-off, can be expected if some specific interactions/reactions occur on the particle surface. Thus, several types of chemical sensors can be designed based on this principle. In this context, the CDs were chosen as a fluorescent probe due to their unique properties, including fluorescent emission and the fact that their particles contain aromatic carbon. Although CDs can provide fluorescent emissions, however, the fluorescent intensity is always weak due to their amorphous structure and the energy relaxation occurring in the radiationless pathway. Thus, the fluorescent emission can possibly be improved by stabilizing the surface of the CDs by some compounds in a core-shell-like structure to reduce the probability of the radiationless pathway. As mentioned in the introduction, in a strong alkaline medium, DA can spontaneously generate PDA, which can coat the surfaces of various materials. From these reasons, the generated PDA is expected to coat the surfaces of CDs due to the strong π-interaction. The fluorescent enhancement expected from this strategy may thus provide an elegant nanoprobe for the detection of DA.

The CDs were chosen as a fluorescent probe due to their unique optical and physical properties. The CDs can be synthesized in one step from the pyrolysis of lactose as a carbon source in an alkali medium. The prepared CDs exhibited fluorescent emission when excited with the appropriate wavelength. The CDs were characterized in detail by several techniques, including fluorescence emission, UV–Visible absorption, TEM, HRTEM, Raman, XPS and XRD. The absorption and emission spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . The absorption spectra exhibit a broad range of 250–500 nm and peak at 278 nm, which can be attributed to the π−π* transition of C=C [33]. In addition, the maximum fluorescence emission was exhibited at 470 nm when excited at 414 nm. The fluorescence spectra exhibited relatively symmetric and narrow bands, implying that the synthesized CDs are nearly homogeneous and monodisperse. The morphology of the as prepared CDs was studied by HRTEM. Fig. 1(b)-(c) and Fig. 1(e)-(f) exhibited HRTEM images of the CDs in the absence and presence of DA, respectively. The synthesized CDs possessed round shape and narrow size distribution with the average particle sized of 2.9 ± 0.6 nm (as shown in Fig.1(d); N = 159). The HRTEM image showed that the CDs possess lattice fringes with interplanar distance around 0.20 nm between them. In addition, a uniform dispersion without apparent aggregation can be observed. From HRTEM images exhibited that the synthesized CDs were of high quality particle characteristics. The XPS experiment was carried out and the results exhibited in Fig. S2(ESI). The C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S2(a)) was resolved into four peaks at 284.50, 284.85, 288.75, and 289.99 eV corresponding to four types of carbon bonds: C=C or C-C, C–O, C=O and COOH, respectively. In addition, for O 1s spectrum (Fig. S2(b)) possessed three peaks at 529.13, 530.84 and 533.08 eV which were assigned to CO, C-O and C=O, respectively. The Raman spectrum was measured and the result is shown in Fig. S3(ESI). A D band (1385 cm−1) and G band (1578 cm−1), two signature peaks for carbon, can be clearly detected for CDs, corresponding to disordered structure in crystalline of the sp2 cluster and the plane graphite sp3, respectively. The XRD pattern (Fig. S4 ESI) displayed a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 23.09° which was ascribed to the (002) lattice spacing of a graphitic-like structure. The results from the spectroscopy and HRTEM experiments strongly indicated that the CDs have been successfully synthesized and can be further applied to fabricate fluorescence nanoprobes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal response and possible sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: DA can be easily oxidized by the oxygen in a strong alkali medium, thus yielding PDA, which possesses weak fluorescent emission, with a peak at approximately 530 nm [34]. As seen in Fig. 2 (a), the fluorescent spectrum of PDA, which was prepared from 200 μM of DA in Tris-HCl pH 8.0, reaches a maximum at approximately 530 nm. However, the fluorescent emission of PDA cannot be significantly observed when using DA at trace concentration levels (10 μM) in Tris-HCl pH 8.0. Thus, the fluorescent spectrum of PDA cannot be directly determined at trace DA levels. However, the peak in fluorescent intensity at 470 nm significantly enhanced when only 10 μM of DA was added into the alkali solution containing the synthesized CDs. It should be noted that the fluorescence spectrum of the prepared CDs itself peaked at 470 nm when excited at 414 nm. This result signified that the detection of DA at the trace level by measuring the fluorescent signal can be performed using the synthesized CDs as a nanoprobe (Fig. 2(a)). In addition, this result confirmed the significant role of CDs in this measurement. In the absence of CDs, no fluorescent signal was detected from the DA in the alkali solution (at the trace DA level). Thus, the turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe can be used for the detection of trace DA using the synthesized CDs.

Here, a possible sensing mechanism was elucidated in detail by several techniques. The possible sensing mechanism is shown in Fig. 3 . The DA is expected to first adsorb onto the CD surface through the strong π-interaction of the CDs and the aromatic moiety of the DA structure. Then, the DA will be oxidized to form a quinone compound and spontaneously be further polymerized to PDA on the CD surface. The fluorescent intensity of the CDs is therefore enhanced due to the formation of a core-shell-like structure that can protect the surface of the CDs and thus reduce the possibility of the radiationless relaxation pathway as a result.

In order to demonstrate the π-interaction between CDs and the generated PDA, the absorption spectrum of the CDs in the presence of DA were investigated as the results showed in Fig. S5(ESI). The results showed that the absorption of the CDs significantly increased with blue-shifted spectra in the present of DA. In addition, the new absorption peak at around 500–600 nm can be observed. This suggested that there are the interaction between CDs and the generated PDA indeed.

To confirm the sensing mechanism, the FT-IR spectra of each compound and their combinations were investigated, as shown in Fig. 2(b). To achieve the best comparison, the PDA was synthesized from a high concentration of DA in NaOH solution. The main characteristic peak of PDA was observed at ∼3500 cm−1, which corresponded to the N-H group. The FT-IR spectrum of the CDs in the presence of DA was different from that of the combined pattern of the CDs and DA. However, a peak at ∼3500 cm−1 was observed when mixing CDs and DA in an alkali medium. This result indicated that the DA was oxidized and yielded PDA on the CD particles. The FT-IR spectrum of the mixing between the synthesized PDA and CDs was also recorded to determine whether it represents the combination patterns of separate particles or not. The result clearly exhibited that the FT-IR spectrum of CDs + PDA was different from that produced by the direct mixing of CDs with DA in an alkali medium. This may imply that the PDA formed on the CD particles instead of forming on the separated particles.

Moreover, the fluorescent lifetime of each system was investigated to understand the sensing mechanism. The fluorescent lifetimes of the CDs, CDs + DA and PDA were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 2(c). The results showed that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs + DA fell between the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and PDA, which confirmed that the new particle was present in the solution and signified that the CDs were coated by the generated PDA. In addition, a longer fluorescent lifetime (compared to the lifetime of the CDs) was observed. This result suggested that the excited electron was stabilized inside the PDA sphere, thus resulting in the higher fluorescent intensity, which corresponded to the fluorescent measurement results shown in Fig. 2(a).

The morphology of the CDs after adding DA was investigated by TEM, and the result is shown in Fig. S1 (ESI). The morphology of the CDs after adding DA was completely different from that of the prepared CDs. The size of the CDs significantly increased with the same spherical and monodisperse shapes. More importantly, a core-shell-like structure was observed in each particle, which suggested that the generated PDA was coated on the CD particles. In addition, Fig. 1(b)-(c) and Fig. 1(e)-(f) exhibited HRTEM images of the CDs in the absence and presence of DA, respectively. The as-prepared CDs have an obvious spacing of the adjacent lattice planes at 0.20 nm, which corresponds to the (102) facet of graphitic carbon [35,36]. In the presence of DA, the CDs were coated with amorphous polymer and remained the spacing of the adjacent lattice planes at 0.20 nm as well as the as-prepared CDs. The results demonstrated that the CDs were coated by the PDA and the fluorescent intensity can be enhanced as the results. All of these results strongly supported that the fluorescent enhancement of the synthesized CDs was due to the generated PDA coated on the CD particles; thus, this observation can be utilized to detect DA by measuring fluorescence at the trace level.

The following parameters were optimized: (a) Sample pH value; (b) reaction time; (c) volume of CDs. Respective data and Figures (Fig. S9–Fig. S11) are given in the Electronic Supporting Information (ESI). The following experimental conditions were found to give best results: (a) Best sample pH value: 8; (b) Optimal reaction time: 20 min; (c) Optimal volume of CDs: 200 μL.

Several principles have been reported to achieve the detection selectivity of DA nanoprobes. However, most of the reported compounds suffer from selectivity over their derivatives, such as norepinephrine and epinephrine, as well as other strong reducing agents. In this work, the detection principle is based on the generation of PDA, which can be coated on the CD surface. In principle, based on the specific characteristic of the generated PDA, there should be fewer possible interfering species. The selectivity of the nanoprobe was therefore studied by measuring the nanoprobe response from 10 μM of ascorbic acid, urea, amino acids, and common sugars. The fluorescence spectra and the corresponding intensities of the CDs in the presence of various possible interfering compounds are shown in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b), respectively. It can be clearly seen that fluorescent enhancement was only induced by DA, while the other tested compounds did not significantly enhance the fluorescent intensity of the nanoprobe. The excellent selectivity of the nanoprobe towards DA over other compounds is caused by the formation of PDA on the CD particles, while other compounds cannot undergo this reaction. This experiment strongly suggests that the nanoprobe possesses excellence selectivity towards the detection of DA and can potentially be used for the determination of DA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the analytical performance of the method
ce:section.ce:para: The method was validated by the determination of its performance parameters to demonstrate its analytical merits under optimized conditions. The experiment was carried out by varying the concentration of DA and measuring the degree of fluorescent enhancement, as shown in Fig. 5 . The result in Fig. 5(a) shows that the fluorescent intensity of the CDs gradually increased with a few spectral red shifts when increasing the concentration of DA. The observed red shift of the fluorescent spectra suggested that the size of the original CD particles was increased due to the generation of PDA coated on the CDs. The degrees of fluorescent enhancement (F-F0) were plot against the DA concentrations, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(b). The degrees of fluorescent enhancement linearly increased as a function of the DA concentration in the range of 1.0–10.0 μM. This linear relationship was expressed by the regression equation of F-F0 = 123.87 [DA, μM] + 5.02, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9946. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) was also evaluated to demonstrate the limitations of the nanoprobe. The LOD was calculated as the concentration of DA that provided a fluorescent intensity equal to 3 × standard deviation of F0. The LOD of the method was found to be 0.22 μM. The LOD at the submicromolar level suggested that the method is a highly sensitive method for DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring DA in a real urine sample and dopamine injection solution
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of using the nanoprobe in a real application, urine sample and dopamine injection solution were chosen as representative samples. This is due to the level of DA in urine may be used to indicate patients treated with selective DA reuptake inhibitors in the clinical treatment of various diseases. The level of DA in urine samples would be helpful in assessing the suitability of patient's drug dosing and compliance, in avoiding overdose, and suggesting a change of therapy in case of ineffectiveness [37]. In addition, the detection of DA in urine sample is a non-invasive method which is an easier way than the measure in blood sample.

The samples and their spiked samples were analyzed by the nanoprobe under the optimized conditions. To demonstrate the accuracy of the nanoprobe, the DA contents in all of the samples and spiked samples were also determined by the HPLC technique. The results from the nanoprobe and the HPLC technique are summarized in Table 1 . These results demonstrated that the nanoprobe can be used to detect the DA content in the dopamine injection solution. The accuracy of the results was confirmed by the analysis of its spiked sample, in which good recovery was obtained, which also signified the accuracy of the concentration. On the other hand, the DA content in the urine sample was not detected by the nanoprobe, suggesting that no DA was present in the urine sample or that the concentration of DA in the urine sample was below the limitation of the nanoprobe. However, after spiking the urine sample with DA, recovery values of 99–108% were obtained, thus confirming the accuracy of the assay. The results from the analysis of real samples indicated that the nanoprobe provided good accuracy and precision. To demonstrate the applicability of this nanoprobe, further experiments were performed to determine DA in the same samples and their spiked samples using HPLC. The results of the analysis using the HPLC technique are listed in Table 1. The results clearly demonstrate that the DA contents in the samples obtained by the nanoprobe agreed very well with those obtained from the HPLC technique. These results confirmed the reliability of using the nanoprobe in real applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance characteristics of the DA nanoprobe with those of other detection platforms
ce:section.ce:para: Several types of chemical sensors have been proposed for the determination of DA based on optical and electrochemical measurements, which are summarized in Table 2 . However, the fluorescent assay has been attractive to analytical chemists because it is highly sensitive. As shown in Table 2, most of the DA probes are based on the measurement of optical properties, such as the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and fluorescent emission of various nanomaterials. Fluorescent-based assays exhibited detection limits that were lower than the micromolar level. Although the detection limit in this work is not much better than those previously reported in the literatures, its selectivity and the simplicity of the method are indeed remarkable. However, the major limitation of this work is the reversibility. The nanoprobe probe can not be reused again due to an irreversible reaction of the generated PDA. Thus, this may enable a single-shot diagnostic assay for disposal sensing approach.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence nanoprobe for DA by utilizing the optical properties of CDs. The nanoprobe can be simply prepared by the pyrolysis of lactose as a carbon source in hot sodium hydroxide solution. The prepared CDs possessed weak fluorescent emissions. However, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was effectively enhanced by trace levels of DA. The sensing mechanism was based on the generation of PDA from the oxidation of DA in an alkaline medium coated on the CD particles. The fluorescent intensities of the CDs increased linearly as a function of increasing DA concentrations, with the lowest detected concentration at the submicromolar level. More importantly, due to its unique sensing mechanism, the nanoprobe did not show a significant response to other common possible interference compounds, such as ascorbic acid, uric acid, sugar and various amino acids. Furthermore, compared to other the optical nanoprobes used for DA detection, the nanoprobe is extremely selective, low in operation cost and very promising for the detection of DA in living systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Thailand Research Fund (RSA6080006 and RTA6080005), the Thailand Research Fund (TRF) through the Royal Golden Jubilee (RGJ) Ph.D. Program (Grant No. PHD/0081/2557) to K.C. and W.N., Research and Technology Transfer Affairs of Khon Kaen University and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Office of the Higher Education Commission, Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.02.030.
